does: This paper systematically investigates causal confusion and reward misidentification in preference-based reward learning, where agents learn reward functions from human preference comparisons. Through sensitivity and ablation studies across benchmark domains, the authors demonstrate that reward models can achieve low test error while failing to generalize to out-of-distribution states, leading to policy failure when optimized. The work identifies three primary failure mechanisms: non-causal distractor features in observations, noise in stated preferences, and partial state observability. The authors develop interpretability methods to diagnose misidentified rewards and show that optimizing such rewards causes policies to exploit spurious correlations, driving behavior outside the training distribution where the learned reward becomes unreliable.
connects: This work builds on established research on causal confusion in reinforcement learning and behavioral cloning, extending these insights to the increasingly popular preference-based learning paradigm. It responds to anecdotal observations of reward hacking and spurious correlations in preference learning systems, providing systematic empirical evidence of these failure modes. The paper connects to broader literature on reward specification, inverse reinforcement learning robustness, and distribution shift in RL. It also relates to interpretability work in machine learning that seeks to understand when learned models capture spurious rather than causal relationships, combining these threads with recent practical applications of preference learning from human feedback in language model alignment and policy customization.
enables: This work establishes a foundation for developing more robust preference-based reward learning methods that can reliably identify causal reward structures. It points toward future work on improved data collection protocols, preference noise modeling, and state representation learning that reduce susceptibility to causal confusion. The interpretability methods introduced could enable better diagnostic tools for practitioners deploying preference learning systems. The identified failure modes motivate research into theoretical guarantees for reward identification under partial observability, causal discovery techniques for reward inference, and robustness improvements in preference learning pipelines. These insights are particularly relevant for high-stakes applications like AI alignment, where reward misidentification poses significant risks.
